facts.text: Oswego’s name comes from the Iroquois word “os-we-go,” meaning “pouring out place.”
The British established a trading post at Oswego in 1722, later fortifying it with a log palisade known as Fort Oswego.
Oswego once hosted three major railroads—the New York Central, Delaware Lackawanna & Western, and New York Ontario & Western—making it a regional rail hub.
The Safe Haven Museum chronicles how 982 Holocaust refugees entered the U.S. through Oswego thanks to President Franklin Roosevelt.
In 1848 Thomas Kingsford founded the Kingsford Starch Factory on Oswego River’s west bank, sparking local industry.
In September 2024 the Lake Ontario National Marine Sanctuary was established off Oswego to protect historic shipwrecks and Native American heritage.
